items.q: What attitude does Pop Art take toward consumer culture?
items.correct: Ambiguous - it both celebrates and critiques mass media imagery.
items.why: Students read Pop Art as either pure celebration or pure protest. Warhol's deadpan repetition of Marilyn or soup cans is deliberately AMBIGUOUS, leaving viewers to decide if it's homage or critique.

items.correct: Visual equilibrium achieved with different elements of equal visual weight on each side.
items.why: Students confuse asymmetry with imbalance. Asymmetrical balance is still BALANCE - achieved through unequal but visually weighted elements (e.g., a small dark shape balancing a large light one).

items.correct: Art that does not depict any recognisable object - exploring form, colour, and line directly.
items.misconception: Art that looks 'a bit like' something but not exactly.
items.random_wrong: Art that doesn't represent any country.
items.why: Students soften the term to 'stylised'. Non-representational means NO reference to objects at all - pure visual exploration (Rothko, Mondrian), distinct from semi-abstract work that simplifies recognisable forms.

items.q: Who created the 'Angel of the North' sculpture installed in 1998?
items.why: Correct answer: Antony Gormley. “Anish Kapoor” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 'Infinity Rooms' are famous immersive installations?
items.why: Correct answer: Yayoi Kusama. “Olafur Eliasson” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Gormley's 'Angel of the North' installed?
items.why: The real figure is 1998. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
